title: HL breadth — sustained investigation across components
summary: This lesson details the Higher Level (HL) extension requirement in IB Visual Arts: demonstrating a sustained investigation that provides breadth and depth across the Comparative Study, Process Portfolio, and Exhibition. It focuses on how to synthesise research, experimentation, and resolved artworks into a coherent, interconnected body of work, a key differentiator for top-band HL students.
learningObjectives: Define 'sustained investigation' and 'breadth' in the context of the IB Visual Arts HL course.
Analyse how the Comparative Study, Process Portfolio, and Exhibition components can be interconnected to form a cohesive body of work.
Articulate explicit connections between critical research and personal art-making practice in written and visual forms.
Apply language from the IB assessment criteria to reflect upon and justify the development of a sustained investigation.
Develop strategies for documenting and presenting a body of work that demonstrates synthesis and coherence, meeting the demands of the HL extension.

simpleExplanation.title: The HL Triptych: Connecting Your Three Canvases
simpleExplanation.summary: The HL Visual Arts course requires you to do more than just complete three separate tasks. You must weave a single, strong thread of investigation through your Comparative Study (research), Process Portfolio (experiments), and Exhibition (final artworks). This sustained investigation demonstrates a deeper, more mature artistic practice.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of yourself as a detective investigating a complex case. The Comparative Study is your background research on similar past cases and criminal methods. The Process Portfolio is your evidence board, where you pin up clues, test theories, follow leads, and document dead ends. The final Exhibition is your presentation in court, where you present a curated selection of the most compelling evidence to prove your case (your artistic intention) to the jury (the examiner).
steps: Identify a core theme or conceptual concern that genuinely interests you. This will be the 'golden thread' that connects everything.
In your Comparative Study, select artists and artworks that directly inform or challenge your core theme, and explicitly state how this research will influence your own studio work.
Use your Process Portfolio to document the journey: show how your research from the CS leads to specific experiments, and how these experiments evolve towards your final artworks.
In your Exhibition, write a Curatorial Rationale that clearly explains the conceptual journey, referencing your research and developmental process to justify your final body of work.

flashcards.back: An in-depth, evolving, and coherent exploration of a central theme, concept, or inquiry that connects the Comparative Study, Process Portfolio, and Exhibition. It is the hallmark of the HL course.
Demonstrating a wider range of technical skills, media, processes, and conceptual concerns than SL. This is evidenced through the body of work as a whole.
The level of detailed inquiry, critical reflection, and sophisticated resolution shown within the sustained investigation. It's about going beyond surface-level exploration.
The successful integration of research, experimentation, and resolved artwork to form a cohesive and meaningful whole. It's the opposite of treating the three components as separate tasks.
The logical and consistent relationship between the different parts of your body of work. A coherent body of work feels unified in its purpose and direction, even if it explores diverse media.

flashcards.front: Exhibition Criterion A: Coherent body of work
flashcards.back: Assesses the student's ability to present a selection of artworks that are connected by concept, theme, or formal qualities, and are supported by a clear curatorial intention.

flashcards.front: Comparative Study Criterion D: Making connections
flashcards.back: Assesses the student's ability to analyse the similarities and differences between artworks and to connect this analysis to their own art-making practice. This is a key link for HL.

flashcards.front: Process Portfolio Criterion A: Skills, techniques and processes
flashcards.back: Assesses the student's experimentation with, and acquisition of skills in, a variety of media and processes. For HL, this should demonstrate greater breadth.

A 700-word (max) statement that explains the intentions of the student artist and the curatorial decisions made in forming the exhibition. It's a critical place to articulate your sustained investigation.
The ideas, themes, intentions, and meanings embedded in an artwork. A strong sustained investigation is driven by clear conceptual qualities.
The degree to which the technical execution of an artwork (use of media, composition, finish) effectively supports and communicates its conceptual intentions.

Implicit connections are hinted at; explicit connections are clearly stated. For the IB, you must make the connections between your research and practice explicit in your writing (PP annotations, CS analysis, Curatorial Rationale).

faq.q: Do my Comparative Study, Process Portfolio, and Exhibition all have to be about the exact same theme?
faq.a: Not necessarily the *exact* same narrow theme, but they must be clearly connected by a sustained line of inquiry. Your investigation can evolve and branch out. For example, an initial interest in 'portraits' (PP) might lead to research on 'identity in post-colonial art' (CS), which then informs an exhibition on 'cultural hybridity' using mixed-media collage. The connection is clear, even though the specific focus has matured and shifted.

faq.q: How do I show 'breadth' if I only want to work in one medium, like painting?
faq.a: Breadth is not just about using different media. Within a single medium like painting, you can demonstrate breadth by exploring a wide range of techniques (impasto, glazing, scumbling), supports (canvas, wood, metal), scales (miniature to mural-sized), and conceptual approaches (abstraction, realism, expressionism). The key is to show you are pushing the boundaries of your chosen medium in a variety of ways.

faq.q: What if my Comparative Study research leads to a dead end in my studio work?
faq.a: This is not a failure; it is a crucial part of a genuine investigation! Document this in your Process Portfolio. A screen that says, 'After researching artist X, I tried their technique of Y, but found it did not convey my concept of Z because... This led me to pivot towards technique A instead...' demonstrates critical reflection and is highly valued by examiners. It shows you are thinking, not just copying.

faq.q: How much of my own artwork should I reference in the Comparative Study?
faq.a: The focus of the CS is the analysis of others' work. However, Criterion D ('Making connections') is specifically about linking that analysis to your own practice. A good rule of thumb is to dedicate a distinct section or a few powerful sentences at the end of each analytical point to explicitly state the influence or connection. Avoid making your own work the main subject; it should be the recipient of the insights gained from the comparison.

sections.content: Welcome to the core of the Higher Level Visual Arts experience. The HL extension is not simply about producing more work than your Standard Level peers; it is about demonstrating a more sophisticated, integrated, and sustained artistic practice. Examiners are looking for evidence of a 'sustained investigation' that provides breadth and depth across all three assessment components. This lesson will deconstruct this requirement and provide you with a clear framework for building a cohesive body of work that excels under the assessment criteria.

At its heart, a 'sustained investigation' is a journey of inquiry. It begins with a central question, theme, or concept that you explore over the two years of the course. 'Sustained' implies that this exploration is ongoing, evolving, and not a series of disconnected projects. 'Investigation' implies a process of research, experimentation, reflection, and resolution. 'Breadth', the specific HL requirement, means you must demonstrate this investigation across a wider range of materials, techniques, and conceptual viewpoints than at SL. You are expected to be more ambitious in your scope.

items: **Sustained Investigation:** A documented journey of inquiry that connects all three components. It should show a clear line of development from initial idea to final resolution.
**Breadth:** Evidenced by exploring a variety of media, processes, and forms. For example, exploring a theme through sculpture, digital media, *and* printmaking.
**Depth:** The rigour of your investigation. This is shown through in-depth research, critical reflection on failures and successes, and the conceptual complexity of your final works.
**Synthesis:** The ultimate goal. This is where your research (CS), process (PP), and outcomes (Exhibition) merge into a single, powerful statement of your artistic practice.

sections.content: The Role of the Comparative Study (CS) as a Catalyst

Too often, students treat the Comparative Study as a separate art history essay. For the HL student, it is the intellectual and critical foundation of your studio practice. The artists and artworks you choose to analyse should be selected because they directly inform, challenge, or resonate with your own developing investigation. The key is to make these connections explicit, particularly in Criterion D: Making Connections. You must articulate *how* analysing another artist's use of colour, material, or composition has influenced a specific decision in your own work.

sections.question: Sample extract from a Comparative Study (Criterion D: Making Connections) for a student investigating 'The Grotesque Body'.
sections.solution: In analysing Jenny Saville's 'Propped' (1992), her confrontational use of scale and the visceral, almost sculptural application of oil paint directly challenged my own initial, more tentative approaches to figure painting. Saville's distortion of flesh, which serves to question idealised forms of beauty, provided a critical precedent for my own work. This analysis prompted a significant shift in my studio practice, documented in my Process Portfolio (screens 11-13), where I moved from precise charcoal drawings to large-scale, expressive oil paintings on unstretched canvas. I sought to emulate her method of building up and scraping back paint to create a sense of scarred, lived-in skin, directly informing the technical resolution of my exhibition piece, 'Carcass I'.

sections.content: The Process Portfolio (PP) as the Narrative

The Process Portfolio is the story of your sustained investigation. It is the connective tissue that binds your research to your final artworks. For HL, your PP must demonstrate a wider breadth of experimentation. It should not be a random assortment of techniques; rather, it should show a purposeful exploration of different media and processes, all in service of your central inquiry. Use your annotations to create a clear narrative. Explain *why* you are trying a new technique, *how* it relates to an artist you studied in your CS, and *what* you learned from the experiment, even if it was a 'failure'.

Create 'connection screens' in your Process Portfolio. Dedicate a screen to a mind map or a short reflective text that explicitly links a CS artist, a series of your experiments, and a developing idea for an exhibition piece. Use arrows and annotations to visually and textually map the journey of an idea. This provides irrefutable evidence of synthesis for the examiner.
The Exhibition and Curatorial Rationale as the Synthesis

Your final exhibition is the culmination of your two-year investigation. The selection and arrangement of works should present a coherent body of work that tells a story. It should not feel like a collection of your 'greatest hits', but a purposefully curated group of works that, together, articulate your central theme. The Curatorial Rationale is your single most important piece of writing to articulate this synthesis. It is your opportunity to guide the examiner through your journey, explicitly stating the conceptual thread that unites the work and referencing the research and experimentation that led to the final outcomes.

sections.question: Sample extract from a Curatorial Rationale for a student investigating 'Urban Decay and Renewal'.
sections.solution: This exhibition, 'Concrete Palimpsest', culminates a two-year investigation into the cyclical nature of urban environments. My inquiry began with a formal and contextual analysis of Gordon Matta-Clark's 'building cuts' (explored in my Comparative Study), which revealed the potential for architecture to be a medium for sculpture. This critical research prompted a series of material experiments, documented in my Process Portfolio, where I explored casting techniques with concrete, plaster, and found urban detritus. The resulting body of work synthesises these threads: the sculptures, such as 'Strata (i-iii)', use layers of cast concrete to echo geological time within the city, while the accompanying photographic series documents the transient beauty of demolition sites. Together, the works aim to present a coherent vision of the city as a living organism, constantly being written over and erased, a concept that has been the sustained focus of my practice.

sections.label: Test your understanding of HL synthesis
